title: A Sentinel-Based Tool Must Treat Mentions Of Its Own Sentinel As Hazardous
problem: Three separate bugs, same root cause: a tool that searches text for a marker or delimiter (JSDoc's */, this repo's LATTICE:BEGIN/END, a literal /** substring) corrupted a file the moment that exact text appeared in prose describing the tool itself, not in the tool's real output. lib/strings-compile.js's glob pattern closed a JSDoc block early. lib/build-lattice-headers.js's own lattice-map.json entry, describing what it writes, confused its own marker search. lib/logger-codes.js had a comment naming this exact hazard class, containing the literal substring '/**', which a bare indexOf('/**') mistook for a real doc comment opener.
exploration: patch each occurrence individually as it's found
ban the tool from ever describing its own sentinel in prose (unenforceable — the tool's own header comment needs to explain what it does)

make the search itself refuse ambiguous matches — require structural context (line-start position, multi-line span) before treating a match as real, and sanitize dynamic content that could contain the sentinel before it's ever written
decision: lib/build-lattice-headers.js now does both: sanitizeForComment() breaks literal */, /*, and the LATTICE:BEGIN/END strings themselves when they appear in JSON-sourced dynamic content (a zero-width space keeps them visually identical while breaking the exact match), and findLineStartDocComment() requires a /** to start its own line and span multiple lines before treating it as a real doc comment — rejecting a same-line mention inside unrelated text.
lesson: A sentinel-based tool's search has no concept of 'inside vs. outside a comment describing the tool' — it is a plain substring match. Any prose the tool itself writes, or any file it processes, that happens to mention the sentinel is a hazard exactly as real as a genuine one. Defend against this before shipping a sentinel-based tool, not after the third occurrence.
impact: Two general-purpose defenses (sanitizeForComment, findLineStartDocComment) now cover every instance of this hazard class found so far, rather than three independent patches for three independent symptoms. Regression-tested in tests/11-lattice-headers.test.js.
